137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**

141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.

205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.

247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.

283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:

136	## Recommendation
137	
138	The slice route is **not the wrong shape for the transport sub-step**, and obstacle #1 is wrong
139	(`Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor`/`pullback` exist). But framing gap1 as "transport a presentation,
140	then `isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_presentation`" mislocates the work: the load-bearing missing
141	pieces are the **`over U ↔ Spec R_r` bridge (C)** and the **section-localization descent (D)**.
142	Build gap1 in this order, keeping slice contact to a single tamed lemma:
143

145	   `M.over U ≅ (restrictFunctor U.ι).obj M` (equivalently land it through
146	   `restrictFunctorIsoPullback`), via the site equivalence `J.over U ≃ Opens(U.toScheme)` induced by
147	   `U.ι.opensFunctor` carrying `R.over U ≅ U.toScheme.ringCatSheaf`. State and prove it with
148	   `set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false` (Mathlib's own `bind` incantation) to

150	   the slice site; everything downstream is geometric.

152	2. **Per-affine local-tilde (P1).** For `r ∈ t` (from `exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData`)
153	   with `D(r) ≤ q.X i`: take `q.presentation i : (M.over (q.X i)).Presentation`; transport to the
154	   smaller open with `Presentation.map` along the **iterated-slice** functor (template:

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).

145	ample line bundle \(L_s\) and coherent sheaf \(\mathcal{F}_s\), it builds the
146	section graded ring (\cref{def:sectionGradedRing}) and module
147	(\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}), records their finite generation

149	(\cref{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}) by combining the graded
150	Hilbert--Serre rationality of the Hilbert series
151	(\cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}) with Mathlib's polynomial-extraction
152	lemma (\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}). The route uses only \(H^0\)
153	(global sections), so it is independent of higher coherent cohomology.

255	  Let \(X_s\) be proper over the field \(\kappa(s)\), let \(L_s\) be an ample line
256	  bundle on \(X_s\), and let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X_s\).

258	  Noetherian graded ring, and the section graded module
259	  \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}) is a finitely
260	  generated graded \(R(X_s, L_s)\)-module. In particular each component
261	  \(\Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a finite-dimensional
262	  \(\kappa(s)\)-vector space.

268	  power \(L_s^{\otimes e}\) is very ample and gives a closed immersion
269	  \(X_s \hookrightarrow \mathbb{P}^N_{\kappa(s)}\). By Serre's theorem the section
270	  graded ring \(R(X_s, L_s)\) is a finitely generated graded
271	  \(\kappa(s)\)-algebra, so it is Noetherian. For the coherent sheaf
272	  \(\mathcal{F}_s\), the same ampleness yields, again by Serre, that
273	  \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
274	  finitely generated graded module over \(R(X_s, L_s)\): the twists
275	  \(\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m}\) are globally generated for
276	  \(m \gg 0\), and the finitely many generating sections in low degrees together
277	  with the multiplication maps generate \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s)\) over
278	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\). Finite dimensionality of each component over \(\kappa(s)\)
279	  follows from coherence and properness (finiteness of cohomology of coherent
280	  sheaves on a proper scheme over a field).

292	  for some \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) and all \(n > N\) the coefficient of \(X^n\) in the
293	  power-series expansion of \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d} \in F\llbracket X\rrbracket\)
294	  equals \(h(n)\):
295	  \[
296	    \exists!\, h \in F[X],\ \exists N,\ \forall n > N : \;
297	    \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1-X)^{-d} \bigr) \;=\; h(n).
298	  \]
299	  Equivalently, the eventual coefficient function of any rational power series
300	  with denominator a power of \(1 - X\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), given by a unique
301	  polynomial \(h\) (Mathlib's \(\mathrm{hilbertPoly}\,p\,d\)). The hypothesis
302	  \([\mathrm{CharZero}\,F]\) is satisfied by \(F = \mathbb{Q}\), which is the
303	  instance used to obtain \(\Phi_{\mathcal{F},s} \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\).
304	\end{lemma}

306	\begin{lemma}[Additivity of \(\dim_\kappa\) on a short exact sequence
307	  (rank--nullity)]
308	  \label{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
309	  \lean{Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank}
310	  \mathlibok
311	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
312	  (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Dimension.RankNullity}).}
313	  Let \(\kappa\) be a field, \(V\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space and
314	  \(W \subseteq V\) a subspace. Then
315	  \[
316	    \dim_\kappa (V / W) + \dim_\kappa W \;=\; \dim_\kappa V .
317	  \]

319	  short exact sequence \(0 \to W \to V \to V/W \to 0\) of finite-dimensional
320	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. This is the additivity that converts the degreewise
321	  short exact sequences of graded components into additive relations among the
322	  coefficients of the Hilbert series.
323	\end{lemma}

346	\begin{lemma}\leanok
347	[Graded Hilbert--Serre: rationality of the Hilbert series]
348	  \label{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational}
349	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational}
350	  \uses{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib,
351	    def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
352	    lem:ratHilb_bump, def:graded_subquotientHilb,
353	    lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
354	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
355	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
356	  % 00K1) (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13893-L13905).
357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
358	  % "Suppose that $S$ is a Noetherian graded ring
359	  %  and $M$ a finite graded $S$-module. Consider the
360	  %  function
361	  %  $$
362	  %  \mathbf{Z} \longrightarrow K'_0(S_0), \quad
363	  %  n \longmapsto [M_n]
364	  %  $$
365	  %  see Lemma \ref{lemma-graded-module-fg}.
366	  %  If $S_{+}$ is generated by elements of degree $1$,
367	  %  then this function is a numerical polynomial."
368	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", Tag 00K1
369	  (proposition on graded Hilbert polynomials); cf.\ [Nitsure], \S 1 (Snapper's
370	  Lemma), [Hartshorne], I.7.}
371	  Let \(R\) be a Noetherian graded ring whose degree-zero part is a field
372	  \(\kappa\) and which is generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra by finitely many
373	  elements, and let \(M\) be a finitely generated graded \(R\)-module with
374	  finite-dimensional graded components \(M_n\). Then there exist
375	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
376	  such that for all \(n > N\)
377	  \[
378	    \dim_{\kappa} M_n \;=\; \bigl[ X^n \bigr]\bigl( p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr);
379	  \]
380	  that is, the Hilbert series \(\sum_{n \ge 0} (\dim_\kappa M_n)\, X^n\) is, up to
381	  a polynomial correction in low degrees, the rational function
382	  \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\), expressed in the power-series form
383	  \(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p) \cdot \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\)
384	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
385	
386	  \emph{Status.} This is the substantive half of the graded Hilbert--Serre
387	  theorem (rationality of the Hilbert series of a finitely generated graded
388	  module). It is not covered by Mathlib: \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}
389	  supplies only the converse polynomial-extraction step from a rational series, not
390	  the production of the rational series from the module. The argument is realised by
391	  an \emph{ambient subquotient induction} (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}):
392	  the whole induction ranges over pairs of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N\) of the
393	  single fixed graded module \(M\), and the Hilbert function is the ambient difference
394	  \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap M_n) - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap M_n)\). The class of such
395	  pairs is closed under the kernel and cokernel of multiplication by a degree-one
396	  element (each presented again as an ambient subquotient pair), so the inductive step
397	  never forms a graded quotient ring \(R/(x)\), a graded quotient module \(M/xM\), or a
398	  regrading of a derived carrier. The genuine Mathlib gap is thereby \emph{avoided}
399	  rather than filled; the residual obligation is the short ambient bookkeeping of the
400	  subquotient blocks (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb},
401	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker},
402	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff},
403	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer},
404	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}), each of which manipulates only ambient
405	  families \(n \mapsto N_{\mathrm{aux}} \cap M_n\) of submodules of \(M\).
406	  % NOTE: The power-series rationality toolkit (\cref{subsec:isRatHilb}) is formalized
407	  %   and axiom-clean. As of iter-020 the present theorem IS a closed, axiom-clean Lean
408	  %   declaration (gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational); the ambient subquotient route
409	  %   (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}) sidesteps missing graded-quotient API by
410	  %   working with ambient families of submodules of \(M\).
411	\end{lemma}
412	
413	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
414	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13938-L13947).
415	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
416	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
417	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
418	%  following short exact sequence
419	%  $$
420	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
421	%  $$
422	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
423	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
424	\begin{proof}
425	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero, lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq,
426	    lem:ratHilb_bump, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
427	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
428	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
429	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb,
430	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
431	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
432	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib,
433	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
434	    \leanok
435	  We argue with the abstract graded \(\kappa\)-algebra
436	  \(R = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} R_n\), whose degree-zero part \(R_0 = \kappa\) is a
437	  field, and the finitely generated graded \(R\)-module
438	  \(M = \bigoplus_{n \ge 0} M_n\) with finite-dimensional components \(M_n\);
439	  there is no appeal to any geometric realisation of \(R\) or \(M\).
440	
441	  \emph{Reduction to generation in degree \(1\).} Since \(R\) is Noetherian and
442	  finitely generated as a \(\kappa\)-algebra, it is generated by finitely many
443	  homogeneous elements of positive degree. Replacing \(R\) by a Veronese
444	  subalgebra \(R^{(e)} = \bigoplus_n R_{en}\) for a suitable \(e\) and re-grading
445	  (and \(M\) by the corresponding Veronese module), we may assume \(R\) is
446	  generated by its degree-one part, \(\kappa[R_1] = R\). The degree-one part
447	  \(R_1\) is then a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space whose \(\kappa\)-basis
448	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) generates \(R\) as a \(\kappa\)-algebra; \(r = \dim_\kappa R_1\)
449	  is the number on which we induct.
450	  % NOTE: The existence of finitely many degree-one generators (the number r the
451	  % induction is on) is expected to follow from the standing hypotheses --
452	  % "finitely generated κ-algebra" together with "generated in degree 1" -- so no
453	  % new hypothesis on gradedModule_hilbertSeries_rational is anticipated. To be
454	  % confirmed when the prover formalizes the assembly: degree-1 generation gives
455	  % R₁ as a finite-dimensional κ-vector space spanning R as a κ-algebra, and a
456	  % κ-basis of R₁ is a finite degree-one generating set.
457	
458	  \emph{Set-up of the ambient data.} Regard \(M\) as the fixed ambient graded
459	  \(\kappa\)-module with internal decomposition \(n \mapsto \mathcal{M}_n := M_n\)
460	  (\cref{def:sectionGradedModule}); the components are finite-dimensional. For each
461	  basis vector \(x_i \in R_1\) let \(t_i : M \to M\) be multiplication by \(x_i\), a
462	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism that raises degree by one (it sends \(M_n\) into
463	  \(M_{n+1}\), \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}). Since \(R\) is
464	  commutative the \(t_i\) pairwise commute, and they trivially preserve the pair of
465	  homogeneous submodules \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\). Because \(R\) is generated by
466	  \(x_1, \dots, x_r\) and \(M\) is finitely generated over \(R\), \(M\) is finitely
467	  generated as a module over the polynomial ring \(\kappa[x_1, \dots, x_r]\) acting
468	  through the \(t_i\); this is the finiteness condition required below.
469	
470	  \emph{Invoking the ambient subquotient induction.} Apply
471	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} to the pair \((N, N') = (\top, \bot)\)
472	  with the \(r\) commuting degree-\((+1)\) endomorphisms \(t_1, \dots, t_r\). It
473	  yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) of order \(r\) for the ambient subquotient Hilbert
474	  function of this pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}),
475	  \[
476	    h(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(\top \cap \mathcal{M}_n) - \dim_\kappa(\bot \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
477	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n - 0 \;=\; \dim_\kappa M_n .
478	  \]
479	  Hence \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa M_n,\, r)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}):
480	  there are \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\), \(d := r \in \mathbb{N}\) and \(N \in \mathbb{N}\)
481	  with
482	  \[
483	    \dim_\kappa M_n \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-d}\bigr)
484	    \qquad (n > N),
485	  \]
486	  which is the claim, with pole order \(d = r\). The internal mechanics of
487	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb} -- the base case via
488	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}, and the inductive step assembling the
489	  kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}), the degreewise
490	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, built on
491	  \(D_5\), \cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) and the finiteness transfer
492	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) into
493	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq} and \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump} -- are the Stacks~00K1
494	  induction realised entirely with ambient homogeneous submodules of \(M\).
495	\end{proof}

497	\subsection{The rationality toolkit \texorpdfstring{\(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\)}{IsRatHilb}}
498	\label{subsec:isRatHilb}
499	% NOTE: Declarations in this subsection are currently \texttt{private} in the Lean
500	% source; \lean{} pins may not resolve until they are moved to a dedicated module.
501	
502	The proof of \cref{lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational} runs the Stacks~00K1 induction
503	on the number of degree-one generators. Each inductive step manipulates the
504	\emph{shape} of the Hilbert series -- a numerator polynomial over a power of
505	\((1-X)\) -- rather than the module itself. We isolate that bookkeeping in a
506	single predicate \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}\) on functions \(f : \mathbb{N} \to
507	\mathbb{Q}\), together with the closure lemmas the induction consumes. These are
508	project-internal helpers realising standard steps of the Stacks~00K1 argument at
509	the level of power series; Mathlib supplies only the converse extraction
510	(\cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}).
511	
512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
513	[Coefficients of \((1-X)^{-1}\) times a series are partial sums]
514	  \label{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul}
516	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
517	  Let \(F \in \mathbb{Q}\llbracket X\rrbracket\) be a power series. Then for every
518	  \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th coefficient of
519	  \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, 1)\cdot F = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\)
520	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}) is the partial sum of the coefficients of
521	  \(F\):
522	  \[
523	    \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl((1-X)^{-1}\cdot F\bigr)
524	    \;=\; \sum_{k=0}^{n} \bigl[X^k\bigr] F .
525	  \]
526	  Equivalently, multiplication by \((1-X)^{-1} = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) is the
527	  summation operator on coefficient sequences. Proved directly in Lean from the
528	  identity \(\operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q},1) = \sum_{m\ge 0} X^m\) and
529	  the Cauchy product.
530	\end{lemma}
531	
532	\begin{lemma}\leanok
533	[Antidifference of a rational Hilbert series]
534	  \label{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalHilbert_antidiff}
536	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
537	  Let \(H, \delta : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\), let \(q \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and
538	  \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). Suppose that for all \(n > N\) the sequence \(\delta\)
539	  is the coefficient sequence of the rational series \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\),
540	  \[
541	    \delta(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\bigr),
542	  \]
543	  and that \(\delta\) is the first difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\), i.e.
544	  \(H(n+1) - H(n) = \delta(n+1)\) for all \(n > N\). Then there is a numerator
545	  polynomial \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) such that for all \(n > N\)
546	  \[
547	    H(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr).
548	  \]
549	  That is, summing a coefficient sequence (anti-differencing) raises the pole
550	  order by one: the partial-sum series of \(q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\) equals
551	  \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\) up to a polynomial correction in low degrees. This is
552	  the power-series heart of the Stacks~00K1 inductive step.
553	\end{lemma}
554	
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
557	  \leanok
558	  Write \(F = q\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\). Multiplying by
559	  \((1-X)^{-1}\) and using the factorisation
560	  \((1-X)^{-(e+1)} = (1-X)^{-1}\cdot(1-X)^{-e}\)
561	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}), \cref{lem:coeff_invOneSubPow_one_mul} gives
562	  \(\bigl[X^m\bigr]\bigl(q\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr) = \sum_{k=0}^{m}[X^k]F\), the
563	  partial sums of \(F\). On the other hand, telescoping the first-difference
564	  hypothesis from \(N+1\) expresses \(H(N+1+j)\) as \(H(N+1)\) plus a window sum
565	  of the coefficients of \(F\). Splitting the partial sum
566	  \(\sum_{k=0}^{n}[X^k]F\) at \(N+2\) and absorbing the resulting constant
567	  discrepancy into a numerator \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\) (a constant function is the
568	  order-\((e+1)\) coefficient sequence of \(C\,(1-X)^{e}\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)} =
569	  C\,(1-X)^{-1}\)), the explicit numerator
570	  \(p = C\,(1-X)^{e} + q\) realises \(H(n)\) as
571	  \([X^n]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-(e+1)}\bigr)\) for all \(n > N\).
572	\end{proof}

574	\begin{definition}\leanok
575	[Rational Hilbert function]
576	  \label{def:ratHilb}
577	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb}
578	  \uses{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
579	  \textit{Source: Stacks 00K1.}
580	  For a function \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(d \in \mathbb{N}\), say
581	  that \(f\) is a \emph{rational Hilbert function of order \(d\)}, written
582	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\), when there exist a numerator polynomial
583	  \(p \in \mathbb{Q}[X]\) and an \(N \in \mathbb{N}\) such that for all \(n > N\)
584	  \[
585	    f(n) \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\bigr)
586	    \;=\; \bigl[X^n\bigr]\bigl(\operatorname{toPowerSeries}(p)\cdot
587	      \operatorname{invOneSubPow}(\mathbb{Q}, d)\bigr)
588	  \]
589	  (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}). This is the ``eventually equal to the
590	  coefficient sequence of a rational series \(p\cdot(1-X)^{-d}\)'' predicate that
591	  the graded Hilbert--Serre induction tracks; its conclusion shape is exactly the
592	  one consumed by \cref{lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib}.
593	\end{definition}
594	
595	\begin{lemma}\leanok
596	[Base case: eventually-zero functions are rational of order \(0\)]
597	  \label{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
598	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.ofEventuallyZero}
599	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
600	  If \(f : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) is eventually zero -- there is
601	  \(N\) with \(f(n) = 0\) for all \(n > N\) -- then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, 0)\)
602	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}). Indeed \(f\) is then the coefficient sequence of the
603	  numerator polynomial \(0\) over \((1-X)^{0} = 1\). This is the base case of the
604	  Stacks~00K1 induction (no degree-one generators). Proved directly in Lean.
605	\end{lemma}
606	
607	\begin{lemma}\leanok
608	[Raising the pole order]
609	  \label{lem:ratHilb_bump}
610	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.bump}
611	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}
612	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
613	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d+1)\): multiplying the numerator by \((1-X)\) cancels
614	  one factor of \((1-X)^{-1}\), so the same coefficient sequence is realised at
615	  pole order \(d+1\). This lets two rational Hilbert functions of different
616	  orders be brought to a common denominator. Proved directly in Lean using
617	  \((1-X)\cdot(1-X)^{-(d+1)} = (1-X)^{-d}\) (\cref{lem:invOneSubPow_mathlib}).
618	\end{lemma}

620	\begin{lemma}\leanok
621	[Closure under difference]
622	  \label{lem:ratHilb_sub}
623	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.sub}
624	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
625	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) and \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, d)\)
626	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}) at the same order \(d\), then
627	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n) - g(n),\, d)\): the numerator of the
628	  difference is the difference of the numerators (over the common denominator
629	  \((1-X)^{-d}\)). Proved directly in Lean.
630	\end{lemma}

632	\begin{lemma}\leanok
633	[Closure under right shift]
634	  \label{lem:ratHilb_shiftRight}
635	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.shiftRight}
636	  \uses{def:ratHilb}
637	  If \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(f, d)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) then
638	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(n \mapsto f(n-1),\, d)\): the right shift on coefficient

644	\begin{lemma}\leanok
645	[Antidifference step for the predicate]
646	  \label{lem:ratHilb_antidiff}
647	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsRatHilb.antidiff}
648	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
649	  Let \(H, g : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{Q}\) and \(e, N \in \mathbb{N}\). If
650	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}) and \(g\) is the first
651	  difference of \(H\) beyond \(N\) (\(H(n+1) - H(n) = g(n+1)\) for all
652	  \(n > N\)), then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(H, e+1)\). This is the predicate-level
653	  packaging of \cref{lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}: a finite difference raises
654	  the pole order by one, the inductive heart of graded Hilbert--Serre.
655	\end{lemma}
656	
657	\begin{proof}
658	  \uses{def:ratHilb, lem:rationalHilbert_antidiff}
659	  \leanok
660	  Unfolding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(g, e)\) supplies a numerator \(q\) and a
661	  threshold \(N_g\) with \(g(n) = [X^n](q\cdot(1-X)^{-e})\) for \(n > N_g\).

/-- **Characterization of `IsIso M.fromTildeΓ` by section localization.** For a sheaf of modules
`M` on `Spec R`, the tilde-Gamma counit `M.fromTildeΓ` is an isomorphism iff for every `f : R` the
section restriction `Γ(M, ⊤) → Γ(M, D(f))` exhibits the target as `IsLocalizedModule (powers f)`.

The forward direction is the affine engine `isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ`; the
reverse is `isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict`. Combined with G1-core
(`isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_isQuasicoherent`, `lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization`, not yet
formalized) — which supplies the right-hand side for any quasi-coherent `M` — this yields gap1
(`lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ`). Project-local. -/
theorem isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict {R : CommRingCat.{u}}
    (M : (Spec R).Modules) :
    IsIso M.fromTildeΓ ↔ ∀ f : R, IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f)
      ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj M).presheaf.map
        (homOfLE (le_top : PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen f ≤ ⊤)).op).hom :=
  ⟨fun h f => by haveI := h; exact isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ M f,
    isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict M⟩

/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — G1-core (Route F) building blocks

The keystone G1-core `lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization` asks: for a *quasi-coherent*
`M : (Spec R).Modules` and `f : R`, the section restriction `Γ(M,⊤) → Γ(M,D(f))` is
`IsLocalizedModule (powers f)`. Via `isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict` this is
*equivalent* to `IsIso M.fromTildeΓ` — i.e. to the statement that a quasi-coherent sheaf on an
affine scheme lies in the essential image of `tilde` (the `QCoh(Spec R) ≃ Mod R` equivalence). That
equivalence is a genuine Mathlib gap at the pinned commit (Tilde.lean only proves the *globally
presented* case, `isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation`).

The composition lemma below discharges the **globally-presented** sub-case end to end (it is the
Route-F endpoint once a global presentation/tilde identification is in hand). The residual gap is
exactly the production of a global presentation/tilde from local (quasi-coherent) data on `Spec R`;
see the handoff in `task_results/.../QuotScheme.md`. -/

/-- **Basic-open restriction localizes, for a globally presented module.** If `M : (Spec R).Modules`
admits a global `SheafOfModules.Presentation`, then for every `f : R` the section restriction
`Γ(M,⊤) → Γ(M,D(f))` exhibits the target as `IsLocalizedModule (powers f)` over `R`.

This is the composition of Mathlib's `isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation` (a global presentation
forces `M.fromTildeΓ` to be an isomorphism, i.e. `M` is a `tilde`) with the affine engine
`isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ`. It is the Route-F endpoint for the
globally-presented case; the general quasi-coherent case additionally requires producing a global
presentation/tilde identification from the (Mathlib-absent) affine `QCoh(Spec R) ≃ Mod R` bridge.
Project-local. -/
theorem isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_presentation {R : CommRingCat.{u}}
    (M : (Spec R).Modules) (_P : M.Presentation) (f : R) :
    IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f)
      ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj M).presheaf.map
        (homOfLE (le_top : PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen f ≤ ⊤)).op).hom := by
  haveI : IsIso M.fromTildeΓ := isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation M _P
  exact isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ M f

/-- **`map_units` field of G1-core (Route F), for any sheaf of modules.** For `M : (Spec R).Modules`
and `f : R`, every element of `Submonoid.powers f` acts invertibly on the sections `Γ(M, D(f))` over
`R`. This is exactly the first field `IsLocalizedModule.map_units` of the target
`isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_isQuasicoherent`, in the shape the 3-field constructor consumes.

It holds for an *arbitrary* `M` (no quasi-coherence needed): on `D(f)` the element `f` already maps
to a unit of the structure ring `Γ(O_{Spec R}, D(f))` (the away-localization
`IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit`), and the `R`-action on `Γ(M, D(f))` factors through it via
the scalar tower `R → Γ(O, D(f)) → Γ(M, D(f))`. Packaged from Mathlib's
`AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.isUnit_algebraMap_end_basicOpen`. Project-local only as the *named* field
of the Route-F decomposition; the substance of G1-core is `surj`/`exists_of_eq` (see handoff). -/
theorem map_units_restrict_basicOpen {R : CommRingCat.{u}} (M : (Spec R).Modules) (f : R) :
    ∀ x : Submonoid.powers f, IsUnit (algebraMap R (Module.End R
      ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj M).presheaf.obj
        (.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen f)))) (x : R)) := by
  rintro ⟨x, n, rfl⟩
  simpa using (tilde.isUnit_algebraMap_end_basicOpen M f).pow n

/-- **Finite basic-open cover refining a quasi-coherent presentation cover.** Given a
sheaf of modules `M` on `Spec R` together with quasi-coherent data `q` (a — possibly
infinite — open cover `q.X : q.I → (Spec R).Opens` of `⊤` with a presentation of
`M.over (q.X i)` on each member), there is a *finite* family of elements `t : Finset R`
whose basic opens cover `Spec R` (`Ideal.span t = ⊤`), with each basic open `D(r)`
(`r ∈ t`) contained in some member `q.X i` of the presentation cover.

This is the topological "finite-cover front" of `lem:exists_isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_cover`:
quasi-compactness of `Spec R` plus the basic-open basis (`PrimeSpectrum.isBasis_basic_opens`)
refine `q.X` to a finite basic-open subcover; the cover condition `q.coversTop` is read off the
`Opens.grothendieckTopology` sieve via `Sieve.mem_ofObjects_iff`, and finiteness is extracted
through `Ideal.span_eq_top_iff_finite`. To obtain `q` from `[M.IsQuasicoherent]`, take

\begin{lemma}
\label{lemma-invert-f-sections}
\begin{slogan}
Sections of quasi-coherent sheaves have only meromorphic singularities
at infinity.
\end{slogan}
Let $X$ be a scheme. Let $f \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
Denote $X_f \subset X$ the open where $f$ is invertible, see
Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-f-open}.
If $X$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated, the canonical map
$$
\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)_f \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{O}_X)
$$
is an isomorphism. Moreover, if $\mathcal{F}$ is a quasi-coherent
sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules the map
$$
\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})
$$
is an isomorphism.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Write $R = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$. Consider the canonical morphism
$$
\varphi : X \longrightarrow \Spec(R)
$$
of schemes, see
Schemes, Lemma
\ref{schemes-lemma-morphism-into-affine}.
Then the inverse image of the standard open $D(f)$ on the
right hand side is $X_f$ on the left hand side.
Moreover, since $X$ is assumed quasi-compact and quasi-separated
the morphism $\varphi$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated,
see Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-quasi-compact-affine} and
\ref{schemes-lemma-compose-after-separated}. Hence by
Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-push-forward-quasi-coherent}
we see that $\varphi_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent.
Hence we see that $\varphi_*\mathcal{F} = \widetilde M$
with $M = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})$ as an $R$-module.
Thus we see that
$$
\Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F}) =
\Gamma(D(f), \varphi_*\mathcal{F}) =
\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f
$$
which is exactly the content of the lemma. The first displayed isomorphism
of the lemma follows by taking $\mathcal{F} = \mathcal{O}_X$.
\end{proof}

theorem Presentation.mapRelations_mapGenerators :
    P.mapRelations F η ≫ P.mapGenerators F η = 0 := by
  simp only [mapRelations, mapGenerators, Category.assoc, Iso.hom_inv_id_assoc,
    ← Functor.map_comp, kernel.condition, Functor.map_zero, comp_zero]

/-- Let `F` be a functor from sheaf of `R`-module to sheaf of `S`-module, if `F` preserves
colimits and `F.obj (unit R) ≅ unit S`, given a `P : Presentation M`, then we will get a
`Presentation (F.obj M)`. -/
@[simps! generators_I relations_I]
def Presentation.map : Presentation (F.obj M) :=
  presentationOfIsCokernelFree (P.mapRelations F η) (P.mapGenerators F η)
    (P.mapRelations_mapGenerators F η) <| by
    refine IsColimit.equivOfNatIsoOfIso (parallelPairIsoMk (mapFree F η _) (mapFree F η _)
      (by simp [Presentation.mapRelations]) (by simp)) _ _ ?_ (isColimitOfPreserves F P.isColimit)
    exact (Cocone.ext (Iso.refl _) <| by rintro (_ | _)
      <;> simp [Presentation.mapRelations, Presentation.mapGenerators, ← Functor.map_comp])

theorem Presentation.map_π_eq :
    (P.map F η).generators.π = (mapFree F η _).inv ≫ F.map (P.generators.π) :=
  (F.obj M).freeHomEquiv.symm_apply_eq.mpr rfl

end

\begin{lemma}[Finite basic-open cover trivializing the $\widetilde{(-)}$-counit (gap1 sub-build)]
  \label{lem:exists_isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_cover}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.exists_isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_cover}
  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_presentation,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
  % NOTE: the Lean decl named in \lean{} does NOT yet exist; this is the irreducible Step-1 ingredient
  % of gap1, the current QUOT prover target. It isolates the only manual content (producing local tilde
  % identifications on a FINITE basic-open cover) from the gap1 gluing assembly.
  % NOTE (iter-029): the TOPOLOGICAL precursor of this lemma — the finite basic-open cover refining a
  % QuasicoherentData cover, WITHOUT the IsIso fromTildeΓ conclusion — IS now formalized axiom-clean as
  % AlgebraicGeometry.exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData (QuotScheme.lean). It needs its
  % own blueprint sub-lemma block (coverage debt). The full IsIso-carrying statement here awaits the
  % per-element presentation transport (modules restriction-to-basic-open + over<->pullback), a
  % Mathlib-absent multi-iter sub-build.
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}, proof,
  % covering step (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L1289-L1303). Cf. Stacks 01HA.
  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
  % "Hence by Modules, Lemma \ref{modules-lemma-quasi-coherent-module}
  %  for every prime $\mathfrak p \subset R$ corresponding to $x \in X$
  %  there exists an open neighbourhood $x \in U \subset X$ such that
  %  $\mathcal{F}|_U$ is isomorphic to the quasi-coherent
  %  sheaf associated to some $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$-module $M$.
  %  In other words, we get an open covering by $U$'s with this property.
  %  By Lemma \ref{lemma-standard-open} for example we can refine this
  %  covering to  a standard open covering.
  %  Thus we get a covering $\Spec(R) = \bigcup D(f_i)$
  %  and $R_{f_i}$-modules $M_i$ and isomorphisms
  %  $\varphi_i : \mathcal{F}|_{D(f_i)} \to \mathcal{F}_{M_i}$
  %  for some $R_{f_i}$-module $M_i$."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Schemes", Lemma \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}, covering
  step of the proof; cf.\ Stacks 01HA.}
  Let \(M\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\). Then there is a \emph{finite} family
  of basic opens \(\{D(g_j)\}_{j}\) with \(\bigsqcup_j D(g_j) = \top\) (their join is the whole
  space) such that for each \(j\) the restriction of \(M\) to \(D(g_j)\) has trivial
  \(\widetilde{(-)}\)-counit: \(\mathrm{IsIso}\bigl((M|_{D(g_j)}).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr)\).
  Equivalently, on each \(D(g_j)\) the sheaf \(M\) is identified with \(\widetilde{N_j}\) for a module
  \(N_j\) over \(R_{g_j} = \Gamma(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R}, D(g_j))\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_presentation,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
  From the quasi-coherence datum (\cref{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}) take the
  cover \(\{X_i\}\) of \(\top\) with local presentations \((M.\mathrm{over}\,X_i).\mathrm{Presentation}\).
  Refine each \(X_i\) to basic opens \(D(g) \subseteq X_i\), which is possible because the basic opens
  form a basis of the Zariski topology (\cref{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}); since
  \(\Spec R\) is quasi-compact (\(\mathrm{CompactSpace}\,(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum}\,R)\)), finitely many
  of these basic opens \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_m)\) already cover \(\top\). On each \(D(g_j)\) the local
  presentation transports across the affine identification \(D(g_j) \cong \Spec R_{g_j}\) (the
  site-\(\mathrm{over}\) presentation maps to a presentation of the restricted sheaf under the
  restriction functor, via \(\mathrm{Presentation.ofIsIso}\) / \(\mathrm{Presentation.map}\)),
  yielding a global presentation of \(M|_{D(g_j)}\) over \(R_{g_j}\). A global presentation forces the
  counit to be an isomorphism (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib}), so
  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\bigl((M|_{D(g_j)}).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr)\) and \(M|_{D(g_j)} \cong
  \widetilde{N_j}\) with \(N_j = \Gamma(M, D(g_j))\); the per-\(D(g_j)\) base identification of
  sections is then \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict} together with
  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_presentation}.
\end{proof}

% --- Mathlib dependency anchors for the gap1 slice-transport route ---

\begin{lemma}[Pullback of sheaves of modules along a morphism of schemes]
  \label{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf}).}
  For a morphism of schemes \(f : X \to Y\), Mathlib supplies the pullback functor
  \(f^{*} : Y.\mathrm{Modules} \to X.\mathrm{Modules}\) on sheaves of modules, left adjoint to
  pushforward, hence colimit-preserving, and carrying the unit sheaf to the unit sheaf. The project
  already uses this functor in the definition of local freeness. It is the geometric restriction
  through which a presentation is transported to an open subscheme.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Restriction of sheaves of modules along an open immersion]
  \label{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf}).}
  For an open immersion \(f : X \to Y\), Mathlib supplies the restriction functor
  \(f^{\mathrm{res}} : Y.\mathrm{Modules} \to X.\mathrm{Modules}\), with
  \(\Gamma(f^{\mathrm{res}} M, U) = \Gamma(M, f(U))\) definitionally; it is a left adjoint (colimit
  preserving) and carries the unit sheaf to the unit sheaf. This is the geometric ``restrict to the
  open \(U\)'' functor on \((\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules}\) — it does exist, contrary to the impression
  that no such functor is available.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Restriction is isomorphic to pullback]
  \label{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf}).}
  For an open immersion \(f\), the restriction functor (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib})
  is naturally isomorphic to the pullback functor (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}):
  \(f^{\mathrm{res}} \cong f^{*}\). This identifies the better-defeq restriction with the
  universal-property pullback, so a presentation transported along one transports along the other.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Transport of a presentation along a colimit-preserving functor]
  \label{lem:presentation_map_mathlib}
  \lean{SheafOfModules.Presentation.map}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent}).}
  Let \(F\) be a functor between categories of sheaves of modules that preserves colimits and is
  equipped with an isomorphism \(\eta : F(\mathrm{unit}_R) \cong \mathrm{unit}_S\) sending the unit
  sheaf to the unit sheaf. Then any presentation \(P : \mathrm{Presentation}\,M\) (a
  cokernel-of-free-modules description) induces a presentation
  \(P.\mathrm{map}\,F\,\eta : \mathrm{Presentation}\,(F(M))\) of the image. Applied to the pullback
  and restriction functors (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib},
  \cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}), it is how a presentation of \(M\) over one site is
  carried to a presentation over another.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Slice-presentation transport for quasi-coherence data]
  \label{lem:quasicoherentData_bind_mathlib}
  \lean{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData.bind}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent}).}
  Mathlib's \(\mathrm{QuasicoherentData.bind}\) transports a presentation across an iterated
  Grothendieck slice: from quasi-coherence data on a cover \(\{X_i\}\) and, for each \(i\), data on a
  cover of \(M.\mathrm{over}\,X_i\), it assembles quasi-coherence data on the composite cover, by
  applying \(\mathrm{Presentation.map}\) (\cref{lem:presentation_map_mathlib}) along the equivalence
  \(\mathrm{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}(\mathrm{Over.iteratedSliceEquiv}\,\cdots)\). It is the
  worked template for the per-element presentation transport used in \cref{lem:over_restrict_iso} and
  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}, taming the slice
  \(\mathrm{IsRightAdjoint}\)/\(\mathrm{HasSheafify}\) synthesis.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Localization is flat]
  \label{lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib}
  \lean{IsLocalization.flat}
  \mathlibok
  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Localization}).}
  If \(S\) is a localization of a commutative ring \(R\) at a multiplicative submonoid, then \(S\) is
  a flat \(R\)-module. Consequently localization is exact and commutes with finite limits; this is
  what lets the section-localization functor pass through the finite sheaf equalizer in the descent
  (\cref{lem:section_localization_descent}).
\end{lemma}

% --- The finite basic-open cover (topological precursor) ---

\begin{lemma}
[Finite basic-open cover refining a quasi-coherence cover]
  \label{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}
  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}
  \textit{The topological finite-cover front of the gap1 transport: any quasi-coherence cover refines
  to a finite basic-open cover.}
  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) and let \(q\) be quasi-coherence data for \(M\)
  (\cref{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}): a — possibly infinite — open cover
  \(\{q.X_i\}\) of \(\top\) together with a presentation of \(M.\mathrm{over}\,(q.X_i)\) on each
  member. Then there is a \emph{finite} subset \(t \subseteq R\) whose basic opens cover \(\Spec R\)
  (equivalently \(\mathrm{span}\,t = (1)\)) such that each \(D(r)\), \(r \in t\), is contained in some
  member \(q.X_i\) of the presentation cover.
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}
  The set \(G = \{\,r \in R : D(r) \le q.X_i \text{ for some } i\,\}\) spans the unit ideal. Indeed
  every prime of \(R\) lies in some member \(q.X_i\) by the covering property \(q.\mathrm{coversTop}\)
  (read off the \(\mathrm{Opens}\) Grothendieck-topology sieve), and the basic opens form a basis of
  the Zariski topology (\cref{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}), so a basic open \(D(r) \subseteq
  q.X_i\) is a neighbourhood of that prime; hence \(\bigsqcup_{r \in G} D(r) = \top\), which is to say
  \(\mathrm{span}\,G = (1)\). A unit-ideal membership uses only finitely many of the generators,
  yielding a finite \(t \subseteq G\) with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = (1)\) and each \(D(r) \le q.X_i\) for
  \(r \in t\).
\end{proof}

% --- (C) The single slice-touching bridge ---

\begin{lemma}[The Grothendieck-slice restriction equals the geometric restriction (gap1, C)]
  \label{lem:over_restrict_iso}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictIso}
  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib,
    lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
  % NOTE: the pinned Lean decl AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictIso does NOT yet exist;
  % it is the single slice-touching ingredient of the gap1 transport. It is to be proved with
  % `set_option backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency false` (Mathlib's own QuasicoherentData.bind
  % incantation) to dodge the slice IsRightAdjoint/HasSheafify synthesis timeout.
  \textit{The one lemma that must touch the Grothendieck slice site; everything downstream of it is
  geometric.}
  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open. The \emph{abstract Grothendieck-slice}
  restriction \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) — an object of the category of sheaves of modules over the
  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) on the slice site \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U\) —
  is canonically isomorphic to the \emph{geometric} restriction
  \((\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota).\mathrm{obj}\,M\)
  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}), equivalently to the pullback \(U.\iota^{*} M\)
  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}), as a sheaf of modules on the small Zariski
  site of the open subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\). The isomorphism is induced by the equivalence
  of sites \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U \simeq \mathrm{Opens}(U.\mathrm{toScheme})\) coming from the opens
  functor of the open immersion \(U.\iota\), under which the sliced sheaf of rings
  \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) corresponds to the structure sheaf of \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\).
  For \(X = \Spec R\) and \(U = D(r)\) a basic open, the target category is
  \((\Spec R_r).\mathrm{Modules}\) under the affine identification \(D(r) \cong \Spec R_r\)
  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
  The open immersion \(U.\iota : U.\mathrm{toScheme} \to X\) induces an equivalence between the slice
  of the opens site over \(U\) and the opens site of \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\): its opens functor is
  fully faithful with essential image the opens of \(X\) contained in \(U\). This equivalence carries
  the sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) to the structure sheaf of
  \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\) and the sliced module \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) to the geometric restriction
  \((\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota).\mathrm{obj}\,M\), which gives the stated isomorphism; the
  comparison with the pullback is \cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}.
\end{proof}

% --- (P1) Per-element local-tilde transport ---

\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherent restricts to a tilde on each basic open of the cover (gap1, P1)]
  \label{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_fromTildeΓ_restrict_basicOpen}
  \uses{lem:over_restrict_iso, lem:presentation_map_mathlib, lem:quasicoherentData_bind_mathlib,
    lem:modules_pullback_mathlib, lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib,
    lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib, lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}
  % NOTE: the pinned Lean decl isIso_fromTildeΓ_restrict_basicOpen does NOT yet exist; it is the
  % per-element transport step of gap1. It replaces the former lem:exists_isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_cover,
  % whose \lean{} pin named a non-existent decl (and whose Mayer--Vietoris framing is superseded by
  % the section-localization descent of lem:section_localization_descent).
  \textit{Per-element step: on each basic open of the refined cover, the restricted sheaf is a
  geometric tilde.}
  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) with quasi-coherence data \(q\), and let \(r \in R\)
  be such that \(D(r) \le q.X_i\) for some member of the cover (as produced by
  \cref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}). Then the restricted counit is an
  isomorphism,
  \[
    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl((M|_{D(r)}).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr),
  \]
  i.e. \(M|_{D(r)}\) is identified with the tilde \(\widetilde{N}\) of the \(R_r\)-module
  \(N = \Gamma(M, D(r))\) on \(\Spec R_r \cong D(r)\).
\end{lemma}
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:over_restrict_iso, lem:presentation_map_mathlib, lem:quasicoherentData_bind_mathlib,
    lem:modules_pullback_mathlib, lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib,
    lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
  The quasi-coherence datum supplies a presentation
  \(q.\mathrm{presentation}\,i : (M.\mathrm{over}\,(q.X_i)).\mathrm{Presentation}\) on the slice over
  \(q.X_i\). Restrict it along the inclusion of opens \(D(r) \subseteq q.X_i\) by transporting across
  the iterated Grothendieck slice — the template is \(\mathrm{QuasicoherentData.bind}\)
  (\cref{lem:quasicoherentData_bind_mathlib}) — using \(\mathrm{Presentation.map}\)
  (\cref{lem:presentation_map_mathlib}) along the colimit-preserving slice functor; this yields a
  presentation of \(M.\mathrm{over}\,(D(r))\). Pushing through the slice-to-geometric bridge
  \cref{lem:over_restrict_iso} converts it into a presentation of the geometric restriction
  \((\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,(D(r)).\iota).\mathrm{obj}\,M\); then, across the affine
  identification \(D(r) \cong \Spec R_r\) (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, by
  \(\mathrm{Presentation.map}\) along the pullback functor \cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}), into
  a genuine global presentation of \(M|_{D(r)}\) as a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R_r\). A global
  presentation forces the \(\widetilde{(-)}\)-counit to be an isomorphism
  (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib}), giving
  \(\mathrm{IsIso}\bigl((M|_{D(r)}).\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\bigr)\).
\end{proof}

% --- (D) The section-localization descent: the gap1 keystone ---

\begin{lemma}[Section-localization descent from a local-tilde cover (gap1 keystone, D)]
  \label{lem:section_localization_descent}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent}
  \uses{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
    lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData, lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib,
    lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib, lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
  % NOTE: the pinned Lean decl isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_descent does NOT yet exist; it is the
  % genuine new content of gap1 and is reused by gap2's section-localization characterization.
  % isLocalizedModule_basicOpen_of_presentation does NOT close it: that lemma needs a GLOBAL
  % presentation of M, whereas here only per-D(r) tilde data is available.
  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", \S "Sections over principal opens",
  % Lemma \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections} (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2156-L2172).
  % Cf. [Hartshorne], II.5.3.
  % SOURCE QUOTE:
  % "Let $X$ be a scheme. Let $f \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
  %  Denote $X_f \subset X$ the open where $f$ is invertible, see
  %  Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-f-open}.
  %  If $X$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated, the canonical map
  %  $$
  %  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)_f \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{O}_X)
  %  $$
  %  is an isomorphism. Moreover, if $\mathcal{F}$ is a quasi-coherent
  %  sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules the map
  %  $$
  %  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})
  %  $$
  %  is an isomorphism."
  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", \S ``Sections over principal opens'',
  Lemma \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections}; cf.\ [Hartshorne], II.5.3.}
  Let \(M\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) (equivalently, by
  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent} and
  \cref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}, \(M\) is locally isomorphic to a
  tilde \(\widetilde{N_j}\) on the members of a finite basic-open cover \(\{D(r_j)\}\) of
  \(\Spec R\)). Then for every \(f \in R\) the section restriction
  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as the localized module
  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\Gamma(M, \top)\); that is, it is
  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\).
\end{lemma}
% NOTE: the lemma STATEMENT is cited to Stacks lemma-invert-f-sections / Hartshorne II.5.3; the proof
% below is a project-internal descent (finite sheaf equalizer over the cover + flatness of
% localization) that deliberately does NOT route through the global affine equivalence (which is
% gap1 itself), so there is no transcribed `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF`.
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
    lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData, lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib,
    lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib, lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
  Refine the quasi-coherence cover to a finite basic-open cover \(\{D(r_j)\}_{j=1}^m\) of \(\Spec R\)
  with each \(D(r_j) \le q.X_{i(j)}\)
  (\cref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}). On each \(D(r_j)\) the restricted
  counit is an isomorphism (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}), so
  \(M|_{D(r_j)} \cong \widetilde{N_j}\) with \(N_j = \Gamma(M, D(r_j))\) a module over \(R_{r_j}\),
  and the basic-open sections of \(M\) below \(D(r_j)\) are the corresponding module localizations
  (\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}).

  intersections presents the global sections as the finite equalizer

      \;\rightrightarrows\; \prod_{j,k} \Gamma(M, D(r_jr_k))\Bigr),
  \]

  (\cref{lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib}, \cref{lem:existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}); the same holds

  \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\) is exact and commutes with this finite limit because localization is flat
  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib}). On each cover piece the localization of
  \(\Gamma(M, D(r_j))\) away from \(f\) is \(\Gamma(M, D(fr_j)) = \Gamma(M|_{D(r_j)}, D(f))\), a
  localization of the tilde \(\widetilde{N_j}\) computed by
  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}; the same on the overlaps. Hence the localized equalizer
  computes \(\Gamma(M, D(f))\), and the comparison map
  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is an isomorphism: the section
  restriction is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\). This is the
  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})\) of Stacks
  \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections} on \(X = \Spec R\), \(X_f = D(f)\), obtained directly from
  local-tilde data without appeal to the global affine equivalence.
\end{proof}
